Diffusion models are a type of latent-variable generative models that are trained to transform Gaussian noise into a sample from a data distribution.
Diffusion models comprise a forward process that gradually annihilates data by adding noise as 'time' t increases from 0 to 1.
Diffusion models comprise a learned generative process that reverses the forward process, starting from a sample of random noise at t = 1 and incrementally adding structure as t decreases to 0.
A conditional diffusion model conditions the steps of the reverse process on labels, text, or an image.
Central to the model is a denoising network fθ that is trained to take a noisy sample yt at some time-step t, along with a conditioning signal x, and predict a less noisy sample.
Using Gaussian noise in the forward process, one can express the training objective over the sequence of transitions as a sum of non-linear regression objectives.
In the process of inference, sampling involves drawing a random noise sample to estimate the noise.
The noise estimated using fθ is used to compute the next latent sample.
